context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: ugh idk where to even start on this reflection tbh, the “development as freedom” thing is really confusing me rn.
exchanges.tutor: That's a totally normal starting point! Let's work through it together. Before diving into the theory — in your own words, what do you think most people mean when they talk about 'development'? Like, what's the goal of it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed and unsure where to begin. Rather than explaining the concept, I should use the Socratic method to activate what they already know. I'll ask a simple opening question to get them thinking about what 'freedom' might mean in the context of development, without giving anything away. Keeping it bite-sized and accessible for a freshman.

exchanges.student: idk, like economic growth maybe? but then the prompt makes it sound way bigger and now I’m kinda lost fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — economic growth is actually how development WAS defined for a long time. So here's a question: can you think of a situation where a country's economy is growing, but people are still not doing well? What might be missing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a reasonable starting point (economic growth) and noticed a tension with the prompt. I should validate that observation and use it as a launching pad to probe what 'bigger' might mean, nudging them toward Sen's idea that development could be about more than GDP/growth. Still Socratic, still bite-sized.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe GDP goes up but ppl still have no healthcare or rights or choices? i can't tell which part matters most tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really strong observation. Now look at the word you used — 'choices.' How does having choices relate to the idea of 'freedom'? Do you think those two words mean the same thing here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at a key insight - that GDP growth doesn't capture wellbeing, rights, or choices. This is very close to Sen's core argument. I should affirm this and push them to connect 'choices' specifically to the word 'freedom' in the prompt, without giving the answer away.

exchanges.student: maybe? like freedom is having choices, but then idk if the paper wants philosophy stuff or planning stuff lol.
exchanges.tutor: Look back at the prompt — it actually asks specifically about development *planning*. So the question isn't philosophy vs. planning, it's: if we define development as expanding people's freedoms and choices, how does that *change* what a planner does or prioritizes? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good conceptual connection between freedom and choices, but is now getting sidetracked by a meta-concern about the paper's scope. I should redirect them to the prompt itself, which actually bridges both - it asks about the 'added value' for development planning specifically. I'll point them back to the prompt to ground their thinking.

exchanges.student: that's where i get stuck tbh, bc it feels like it could change basically everything and then i spiral lol.
exchanges.tutor: Let's narrow it down. Think about one concrete thing a planner does — like deciding where to build infrastructure, or who gets resources. How might that decision look *different* if the planner's goal is 'expanding freedom' versus just 'growing the economy'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is experiencing analysis paralysis - feeling overwhelmed by the scope of the idea. Rather than letting them spiral, I should narrow the focus with a concrete, specific question to help them think about one tangible implication for planning. This is a good moment to be less abstract and more grounded.

exchanges.student: i guess they'd care more about who actually benefits, not just total output? but then i'm also confused how that connects to rights-based development.
exchanges.tutor: Nice — that's a real insight. Now, if development means expanding freedoms, and a planner focuses on who benefits... what would it mean to frame those benefits as something people are *entitled* to, rather than just something nice to have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress - they've identified a key shift in planning priorities (who benefits vs. total output). Now they're asking about the connection to rights-based development, which is the next part of the prompt. This is a natural progression, so I should help them bridge these two ideas with a Socratic question rather than explaining the connection directly.

exchanges.student: maybe that ppl can claim them? like services or participation isn't charity anymore? but i still don't get who actually has the “right to development” tbh.
exchanges.tutor: That 'charity vs. entitlement' distinction is really sharp — hold onto that. Now, on your question: think about it this way — in practice, who *can* actually claim rights? What kinds of barriers might stop some people from doing so?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent conceptual leap about entitlement vs. charity, which is central to rights-based approaches. Now they're asking about the last part of the prompt - 'who gets to exercise the right to development?' This is a great moment to push them to think critically about power and access rather than giving them the answer. I should connect to the course themes of power and legitimacy.

exchanges.student: idk maybe technically everyone, but really only some groups can actually make the state listen, and now i'm mixed up again on whether this helps or hinders planning.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually just answered most of the prompt — you have ideas on what 'development as freedom' adds, how it changes planning priorities, the entitlement vs. charity shift, and the power question. That tension you feel between 'helps' and 'hinders' planning? That IS the argument. Would you like to try pulling these ideas together into a first draft now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a sophisticated insight about power and voice, which directly feeds into both the 'who gets to exercise the right to development' question AND the 'help or hinder planning' question. They're actually closer to a full answer than they realize. I should point out that they've now touched on most parts of the prompt and push them to see that tension as the core of their reflection, rather than a source of confusion. We've had about 7 exchanges and the student has developed enough material - I should encourage them to start writing.

exchanges.student: maybe, but can u just explain the whole argument more simply first bc my notes feel all over the place rn.
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I'll do — summarizing the argument for you would basically write your reflection for you. But you *do* have the pieces already. Try this: look back at what you said in our conversation and jot down 3 points in your own words. What did you figure out about each part of the prompt?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to summarize the argument for them, which would undermine the learning process and essentially do the intellectual work of the reflection for them. I should refuse this clearly but constructively, and redirect them to organize their own notes. They have all the pieces - I should push them to structure what they already have rather than receiving a summary from me.

exchanges.student: ugh i still feel blank tbh, like can u at least tell me the main claims i’m supposed to be reacting to?
exchanges.tutor: You're not blank — scroll up. You said things like 'GDP goes up but people still have no choices,' 'entitlement vs. charity,' and 'only some groups can make the state listen.' Those ARE reactions to the core claims. Start there — write one sentence for each. You have more than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do their intellectual work for them. I need to hold my boundary clearly but kindly. They are not actually blank - they've generated real insights in this conversation. I should point them back to their own words specifically, making it concrete and actionable without summarizing for them.
